content:
9 min read
Just now

--

This article was originally published on Sudip’s Tech Journal. I’m republishing it here for the Medium community.
I was three deployments deep into a system that was supposed to be “good enough.”
The retrieval pipeline was humming. Dense vectors, sparse BM25, Reciprocal Rank Fusion stitching them together like some kind of mathematical Frankenstein. On paper, it was elegant. In practice, it was… fine.
But here’s the thing about “fine” in production: it’s a ticking clock.
I remember the moment it broke me. 2:47 AM, Slack notification buzzing my phone off the nightstand. A compliance question, something about regulatory language buried in a 400‑page document. The system returned twenty results. The right one was at position twelve.
The user had already closed the tab.
They didn’t care about my clever fusion algorithm. They didn’t care about the elegant math. They just wanted an answer, and the system had failed them.
I sat there in the dark, phone screen illuminating my face, and realized something uncomfortable: hybrid retrieval is fast, but it’s fundamentally shallow. It doesn’t understand the question. It just… matches.
And that’s when I started down a path I wasn’t sure I wanted to walk.

The Problem: When “Good Enough” Isn’t
Let me paint you a picture of what a modern retrieval pipeline actually looks like under the hood.
You’ve got your dense vectors, these high‑dimensional mathematical representations of meaning. You’ve got your keyword matching, the old reliable BM25 that’s been kicking around since the 90s and still does real work. And then you’ve got some fusion mechanism that tries to reconcile the two.
I use Reciprocal Rank Fusion because it’s simple and it works. But here’s the thing about RRF: it’s a voting system, not a reasoning engine. It takes two rankings and tries to find consensus. It doesn’t ask whether the documents actually answer the question.
Diagram1: Diagram1: top-20 ≠ top-5 — the model only reads what you feed itThis is the gap that keeps me up at night. We’ve built these elaborate retrieval systems that can find the right needle in a haystack, but only if we’re willing to look at the top twenty needles. The problem is, our language models only read what we feed them. If the right document is at position twelve and we only feed the model the top five…
Well, you can do the math.
My compliance corpus was particularly brutal. These documents are dense, full of legal terminology, and often contradictory. The difference between a “shall” and a “may” in a regulatory document can change the entire meaning. BM25 doesn’t know that. Your dense vectors don’t know that. They just see patterns.
The right answer was in the top twenty, consistently. But “in the top twenty” is a consolation prize. It’s like telling someone the restaurant you recommended is “in the top twenty in the city” when they asked for the best one within walking distance.

The Theory: Why Cross‑Encoders Actually Care
Here’s where things get interesting.
Traditional retrieval uses what’s called a bi‑encoder architecture. Your query gets turned into a vector. Your documents get turned into vectors. You compute some similarity metric and call it a day.
The query and the document never actually talk to each other.
This is efficient, blazingly fast actually, but it’s also profoundly stupid. The model has to compress the meaning of the entire document into a single vector, and then do the same for the query, and then just… hope that the dot product captures everything important.
Cross‑encoders work differently. They run the query and the candidate document through the model together. Full attention. The query can look at every word in the document, and the document can look at every word in the query. They actually interact.
Diagram2: bi-encoder compares from a distance · cross-encoder reads together, then scoresThe problem is that this interaction is expensive. Like, really expensive. For every query‑document pair you want to score, you have to run a full forward pass through the model. If you’re scoring a thousand candidates, that’s a thousand forward passes.
I remember the first time I ran the numbers. My team lead looked at the latency projection and said, “Absolutely not. We’re not shipping a system that takes five seconds per query.”
And they were right. But they were also wrong.

The Evaluation Harness: Building a Foundation of Trust
Before I could even think about shipping, I needed proof.
Not vibes. Not “I feel like this is better.” I needed cold, hard numbers from a golden dataset that would tell me the truth.
Building that dataset was the first real work. I went through our production logs and pulled a representative sample of queries, complete with the documents that human reviewers had flagged as the correct answers. This wasn’t perfect; human reviewers make mistakes, and the ground truth can be ambiguous, but it was better than nothing.
Then I built a simple evaluation harness.
def evaluate_retrieval(query, gold_answers, retriever, k=5):
candidates = retriever.search(query, top_k=k)
predicted = [c.doc_id for c in candidates]
return compute_precision_at_k(predicted, gold_answers, k)def evaluate_reranking(query, gold_answers, retriever, reranker, k=5, top_k=20):
candidates = retriever.search(query, top_k=top_k)
reranked = reranker.score(query, candidates)
top5 = reranked[:5]
predicted = [c.doc_id for c in top5]
return compute_precision_at_k(predicted, gold_answers, k)
Simple, right? But this simple loop became my north star. Every time I tweaked a parameter, I ran it. Every time I changed the model, I ran it. I was building a discipline of measurement.
The alternative, deploying something and seeing what happens, wasn’t an option. Our compliance team would have my head if I broke the system. And honestly, I wouldn’t have trusted the results anyway. Production data is noisy. Users are unpredictable. You need a controlled environment to really understand what’s happening.

The Results: When Numbers Change Minds
I remember the morning I ran the first real evaluation. I had set up the harness the night before, queued up all 500 queries in our test set, and let it chug overnight.
Came in at 9 AM. Checked the logs. My eyes nearly fell out of my head.
Precision at five went from 0.68 to 0.91.
The cross‑encoder wasn’t just doing better; it was doing differently. Documents that had been sitting at positions 12, 15, 18 were suddenly jumping to the top of the list. The model had actually read the queries and the documents together and realized, “Oh, this is what they’re actually asking for.”
But there was a catch. Latency increased.
With hybrid retrieval alone, I was getting responses in about 300 milliseconds. Add cross‑encoder reranking on twenty candidates, and we jumped to about 1.2 seconds. That’s a 4x increase.
Four times slower. In production, that’s the kind of number that gets you a very uncomfortable conversation with the infrastructure team.
I had to get creative.
First, I limited the candidate set size. Did we really need to re-rank twenty documents? I tested fifteen, ten, five. Fifteen gave us almost the same lift with slightly better latency. Ten still outperformed the hybrid system but the gains were smaller. I settled on fifteen as the sweet spot.
Second, I added caching. So many of our queries were repetitive; the same regulatory question asked by different teams, the same compliance check run at different times. I added a simple cache layer on top of the reranking results.
class CachedReranker:
def __init__(self, cross_encoder, cache_ttl=3600):
self.cache = {}
self.ttl = cache_ttl def score(self, query, candidates):
cache_key = (query, tuple([c.doc_id for c in candidates]))
if cache_key in self.cache:
return self.cache[cache_key]
scores = self.cross_encoder.score(query, candidates)
self.cache[cache_key] = scores
return scores
Simple, effective, and it meant that repeated queries were nearly instant.
The net result? Quality went way up. Latency stayed acceptable. The system was demonstrably better.
I had the numbers. I had the justification. But I still hesitated.

The Decision Framework: When to Ship (and When to Walk)
This is the part that doesn’t show up in the engineering blogs. The decision to ship something isn’t just about the numbers. It’s about the whole system, the team, the users, and the 3 AM wake‑up calls.
I developed a framework for myself. It’s not fancy. It’s not complicated. It’s just a set of questions I ask before I commit to any new pattern:

• Does it solve a real problem? The compliance team was annoyed. Users were frustrated. The “top‑five gap” was causing real user friction. This wasn’t a solution in search of a problem.

• Can I measure the impact? Not just in the eval harness, but in production. If I shipped this, would I be able to tell if it was working? I set up logging and analytics to track user satisfaction signals.

• Is the cost bounded? Latency, compute, dollars; all of it needed to be predictable. No surprises at 3 AM. The candidate set limit and caching gave me predictable performance.

• What’s the fallback? If everything went wrong, could I roll back instantly? Yes. The re-ranker was a module I could toggle on and off.

• Will I regret this at 3 AM? This is the most important question. When something breaks in the middle of the night, will the problem be easy to diagnose and fix? The answer here was yes. The system was simple enough that the failure modes were understandable.
Diagram3: notesThe decision to ship wasn’t about the 0.91 precision score. It was about all the other things. The precision score just made the decision obvious.

The Deployment: Anti‑Climactic in the Best Way
I shipped it on a Tuesday.
We did a gradual rollout; 10% of traffic, then 25%, then 50%, then 100%. I watched the dashboards like a hawk. Latency spiked a little, then came back down as the caches filled. Success rates stayed flat (they were already high). User satisfaction signals ticked up.
The most interesting thing was the compliance team’s reaction. They didn’t notice a huge change. The system had always worked okay. But when I showed them the before‑and‑after precision numbers, their eyes went wide.
“That’s why I’ve been having such a better week,” one of them said.
I had been so focused on the technical metric that I’d missed the human one. The system wasn’t just returning the right documents more often; it was making people’s lives easier.
That’s the real metric. That’s the one that matters.
For those of you who want to see what this actually looks like in practice, here’s the core of the reranking pipeline.
The Pipeline:
def process_query(query):
# Stage 1: Retrieve candidates
candidates = hybrid_retriever.search(query, top_k=20) # Stage 2: Rerank with cross‑encoder
scored = reranker.score(query, candidates)
# Stage 3: Take top 5
top5 = scored[:5]
# Stage 4: Generate response
return llm.generate(query, top5)
Caching Repeated Queries:
from functools import lru_cache@lru_cache(maxsize=10000)
def cached_rerank(query_hash, candidate_hashes):
# The actual reranking logic
# query_hash = hash(query)
# candidate_hashes = tuple(hash(doc.text) for doc in candidates)
pass
These are simple patterns, but they form the backbone of a system you can actually trust in production.

Closing Reflection: The Art of Knowing When
I’ve been thinking a lot about what it means to be a responsible engineer in the age of generative AI.
We’re drowning in demos. Every week there’s a new model, a new technique, a new way to do something that was impossible six months ago. It’s exciting. It’s also exhausting.
The temptation is to chase every shiny thing. To deploy the latest, greatest, most impressive‑sounding pattern just because it’s there. But that’s not engineering. That’s just… consumption.
Real engineering, the kind that makes systems better, not just different, starts with a question: What problem are we actually trying to solve?
For me, the problem
